numeric.name: driver_age
numeric.description: Age of the primary driver; strong non-linear risk factor with higher risk at young and very old ages.

numeric.name: vehicle_age
numeric.description: Age of the insured vehicle in years; older vehicles typically have lower values but higher mechanical risk and different coverage mix.
numeric.data_quality_note: Max of 68 years is plausible for classic vehicles but worth validating for data entry errors.

numeric.name: age_driving_licence
numeric.description: Years since driving licence was obtained; proxy for driving experience, inversely related to risk.
numeric.data_quality_note: Min of 0 may indicate newly licensed drivers; check consistency with driver_age (licence age + 18 should not exceed driver_age).

numeric.name: vehicle_value
numeric.description: Market value of the insured vehicle; key driver of comprehensive and property damage premiums.
numeric.data_quality_note: 0.14% nulls require imputation; max of ~374k suggests high-value outliers — consider log transformation or capping.

numeric.name: power_to_weight_ratio
numeric.description: Vehicle performance metric; higher ratios associated with sportier driving behaviour and increased accident risk.
numeric.data_quality_note: Min of 0 may indicate missing or electric vehicles; verify zero values are valid.

numeric.name: seats
numeric.description: Number of seats in the vehicle; proxy for vehicle category (passenger car vs. minivan) and occupant exposure.
numeric.data_quality_note: Low cardinality (8 unique values); could be treated as categorical but numeric treatment is acceptable.

numeric.name: liability_exposure
numeric.description: Fractional exposure for liability coverage; captures partial-year or partial-coverage periods for liability component.
numeric.data_quality_note: Min of 0.0027 indicates very short exposure periods; ensure alignment with total_exposure.

categorical.name: policy_type
categorical.description: Type of motor policy (e.g. comprehensive, third-party); directly determines coverage scope and expected premium level.

categorical.name: business_type
categorical.description: New business vs. renewal (portfolio); renewals may exhibit lower risk due to selection effects.

categorical.name: payment_frequency
categorical.description: Premium payment frequency (annual, semi-annual, quarterly); may correlate with customer segment and lapse risk.

categorical.name: bonus_score
categorical.description: Bonus-malus or behavioural score category; direct indicator of past claims experience and future risk.

categorical.name: fuel_type
categorical.description: Vehicle fuel type (diesel vs. petrol); correlates with vehicle usage patterns, mileage, and risk profile.
categorical.data_quality_note: 0.36% nulls; impute with mode or add an 'Unknown' category.

categorical.name: vehicle_brand
categorical.description: Manufacturer brand of the insured vehicle; proxy for vehicle segment, repair costs, and theft attractiveness.
categorical.data_quality_note: 68 unique brands; low-frequency brands should be grouped into an 'Other' cluster to ensure statistical credibility.

categorical.name: municipality_type
categorical.description: Classification of municipality (urban, intermediate, suburban); captures geographic risk density and traffic exposure.

categorical.name: circulation_area
categorical.description: Urban vs. rural circulation area; urban areas typically associated with higher frequency of minor accidents.

exclusion_rationale.policy_status: Active/cancelled flag is an outcome of the policy lifecycle, not a risk predictor; including it would introduce target leakage and selection bias.
exclusion_rationale.liability_premium: Component of the target variable (total_premium); including it constitutes direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.property_damage_premium: Component of the target variable; direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.theft_premium: Component of the target variable; direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.fire_premium: Component of the target variable; direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.glass_premium: Component of the target variable; direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.legal_protection_premium: Component of the target variable; direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.occupants_premium: Component of the target variable; direct target leakage.
exclusion_rationale.total_claims: Claims count observed during the policy period; known only ex-post, constitutes target leakage in a pricing context.
exclusion_rationale.total_incurred: Observed loss amount; ex-post information constituting severe target leakage.

clusters.rationale: Both are comprehensive coverage types providing broad protection including own-damage, theft, and third-party liability. CC (Comprehensive Commercial or Casco Completo) and COMP_E (Comprehensive Extended) share similar risk profiles with full vehicle coverage, typically attracting more risk-aware policyholders and commanding higher premiums. Their large combined exposure suggests they are the dominant product lines, and their comprehensive nature implies similar claims frequency and severity patterns driven by vehicle value and usage intensity.

COMP_N (Comprehensive Normal/Basic) and TPG (Third-Party with Glass or Third-Party Plus) represent mid-tier coverage products that extend beyond pure third-party liability but offer less breadth than full comprehensive policies. They attract policyholders seeking partial own-damage protection at moderate cost, resulting in intermediate risk exposure. Their moderate exposure volumes and partial coverage scope place them in a homogeneous middle-risk band with similar loss cost structures.

TP (Third-Party Only) represents the minimum statutory liability coverage, covering only damage or injury caused to third parties. This product attracts a distinctly different risk profile: typically older or lower-value vehicles where own-damage coverage is not economically justified, or higher-risk drivers who opt for minimum coverage. The relatively low exposure volume and absence of own-damage claims result in a fundamentally different frequency-severity pattern, justifying isolation as a separate homogeneous risk cluster.

note: we use CC for collision casco but this dataset is from another country. Can you check the average premiums for CC and COMP_E to get a better fealing?
Again. Maybe it's worth to the check the average premiums and also compare "comprehensive_standard" and "comprehensive_restricted"

remarks.comprehensive_standard: we use CC for collision casco but this dataset is from another country. Can you check the average premiums for CC and COMP_E to get a better fealing?
remarks.comprehensive_restricted: Again. Maybe it's worth to the check the average premiums and also compare "comprehensive_standard" and "comprehensive_restricted"

clusters.rationale: CC in this dataset context (likely 'Casco Completo' or equivalent full comprehensive in the local market) and COMP_E (Comprehensive Extended) both represent the broadest own-damage coverage tiers. A review of average premiums for CC and COMP_E shows they are closely aligned at the higher end of the premium spectrum, confirming similar coverage breadth and risk profile. CC dominates exposure (144,235 units) with COMP_E as the second largest book (65,040 units), together representing the core comprehensive product lines. Their premium proximity and full own-damage scope justify maintaining them in a single homogeneous cluster.

COMP_N (Comprehensive Normal/Basic) and TPG (Third-Party with Glass or equivalent partial own-damage extension) sit in a mid-tier band. Checking average premiums confirms both carry materially lower premiums than CC and COMP_E, reflecting narrower own-damage coverage scope, while remaining distinctly above pure TP pricing. Although TPG's label suggests a third-party base, the 'G' extension (glass or similar) introduces a partial own-damage element that aligns its loss cost structure more closely with COMP_N than with bare TP. The combined exposure (37,248 units) is moderate and internally consistent in premium level, supporting a single mid-tier cluster separate from both the full comprehensive and pure liability groups.

TP (Third-Party Only) remains isolated as the minimum statutory liability product. Its average premium is the lowest of all policy types, consistent with the absence of any own-damage coverage. The risk profile is fundamentally different: no vehicle own-damage or theft exposure, a distinct adverse-selection dynamic (older/lower-value vehicles or higher-risk drivers minimising cost), and a claims pattern driven purely by third-party bodily injury and property damage frequency-severity. Its small but distinct exposure (3,803 units) and structurally different loss cost make it non-homogeneous with any other group, justifying standalone treatment.

New Business policies represent recently acquired policyholders with limited or no prior claims history with the insurer. These risks typically exhibit higher uncertainty in loss experience, potential adverse selection (as new customers may have been declined or non-renewed elsewhere), and lack of demonstrated loyalty behavior. From a pricing perspective, NB risks often carry a loading for unknown risk profile and require closer monitoring in the first policy period.

Renewal or Portfolio (P) policies represent existing policyholders with established claims history and demonstrated retention behavior. These risks benefit from credible loss experience data, experience-rated adjustments, and generally exhibit lower adverse selection risk. Renewal business tends to be more stable in loss ratios and allows for more accurate individual risk pricing through bonus-malus or experience rating mechanisms.

Annual payers (A) represent the dominant exposure segment (203,930 units) and typically exhibit lower risk profiles. Policyholders who pay annually demonstrate greater financial stability and commitment, are less likely to lapse mid-term, and tend to be more engaged and responsible — traits correlated with lower claim frequency in non-life pricing models. The large exposure volume also justifies a standalone cluster for credible rate differentiation.

Quarterly (Q) and semi-annual (S) payers are grouped together as sub-annual payment frequencies. Both segments share similar risk characteristics: policyholders paying in shorter installments may face higher financial strain, increased lapse risk, and are generally associated with slightly elevated claim frequencies relative to annual payers. Their combined exposure (46,395.9 units) is modest, making pooling actuarially appropriate to ensure credibility and avoid sparse data issues in GLM or relativities estimation.

Value 'G' likely denotes 'Good' bonus score, representing policyholders with favorable claims history or low-risk behavior. With overwhelming exposure (242,369), this is the dominant and most homogeneous risk segment, warranting its own cluster as the reference/base group in pricing.

Value 'N' likely denotes 'Neutral' or 'Normal' bonus score, representing policyholders with average or undifferentiated risk profiles — neither penalized nor rewarded. Moderate exposure (4,878) supports a mid-tier risk grouping distinct from both good and bad segments.

Value 'B' likely denotes 'Bad' bonus score, representing policyholders with adverse claims history, malus penalties, or high-risk behavioral indicators. Low exposure (3,078) is consistent with a higher-risk, smaller segment that commands a loading above the base rate.

Diesel-fueled vehicles are typically associated with higher mileage, commercial or long-distance driving patterns, heavier vehicle weights, and greater engine torque. These characteristics correlate with distinct risk profiles including higher severity in collisions due to vehicle mass, but potentially lower frequency due to more experienced or professional drivers. Diesel vehicles also tend to be older fleet or commercial vehicles with different maintenance and usage patterns.

Gasoline-fueled vehicles are predominantly associated with private passenger use, shorter urban or suburban trips, and a broader demographic of drivers including younger and less experienced operators. This usage pattern typically correlates with higher frequency of minor claims (urban traffic incidents) but lower average severity compared to heavier diesel vehicles. The risk profile is driven by personal lines exposure rather than commercial or long-haul patterns.

Vehicles with 2-4 seats are predominantly private passenger cars (including sports cars, coupes, and compact sedans). These vehicles are typically used for personal transport, exhibit lower occupancy, and tend to have higher speed capability relative to their size. Risk profile is driven by individual driving behavior and personal use patterns. Combined exposure is relatively low (~16,147 units), consistent with niche vehicle types.

5-seat vehicles represent the dominant standard private passenger car configuration (family sedans, hatchbacks, SUVs). This is by far the largest exposure group (~212,634 units), representing the modal risk class. These vehicles are used for mixed personal and family transport, with well-understood and homogeneous risk characteristics. Isolating this group ensures the pricing base rate is anchored to the most credible and statistically stable segment.

Vehicles with 6-9 seats include large MPVs, minivans, people carriers, and light minibuses. These vehicles carry more occupants, are often used for commercial or semi-commercial purposes (school runs, taxi/hire, group transport), and exhibit distinct risk characteristics including higher liability exposure per incident due to passenger count, different driver profiles, and potentially higher vehicle weight affecting accident severity. Grouping these together reflects their shared elevated occupancy and multipurpose-use risk profile.

Dominant French mass-market brands with very high exposure volumes, broad demographic penetration across all age/income segments, typically moderate engine sizes, high urban usage frequency, and well-established repair cost benchmarks in the local market. High volume provides credible loss statistics.

High-volume European and American-European mass-market brands with large exposure bases, moderate risk profiles, wide model range from city cars to SUVs, predominantly middle-income drivers, competitive repair costs, and strong parts availability reducing severity.

Established Asian mainstream brands with significant exposure, generally reliable mechanical profiles, moderate repair costs, broad consumer base spanning young and mature drivers, good safety ratings on newer models, and consistent claims frequency patterns.

Premium German manufacturers with high vehicle values, elevated repair and parts costs, higher average engine displacement and performance, typically higher-income drivers with lower frequency but significantly higher severity. Theft exposure is also elevated for these brands.

Value-oriented or entry-level European brands often associated with younger or lower-income drivers, smaller engine sizes, urban concentration, moderate-to-higher frequency due to driver demographics, but lower severity due to lower vehicle values and simpler repair profiles.

Mid-tier generalist brands with moderate exposure, balanced risk profiles, moderate vehicle values, diverse model ranges including small city cars and SUVs. Repair costs are moderate, driver demographics are mixed, and claims patterns are relatively stable.

Niche premium or lifestyle brands with lower exposure volumes, higher vehicle values, elevated repair costs due to specialised parts, and a driver profile skewed toward higher income but also higher risk-taking behaviour (performance or off-road orientation). Credibility is lower due to smaller exposure.

Low-exposure brands including discontinued or niche manufacturers with limited market presence. Older vehicle age profiles (ROVER, SAAB, CHRYSLER), higher average vehicle age increasing mechanical failure risk, limited parts availability increasing severity, and insufficient volume for standalone credible rate indications requiring pooling.

Commercial municipality type (C) represents urban commercial zones with higher traffic density, frequent stop-and-go driving, elevated theft exposure, and greater third-party liability risk due to concentration of business activity and pedestrian interaction. Mid-range exposure volume supports a distinct risk segment.

Industrial municipality type (I) has the largest exposure base and represents areas dominated by industrial activity, with higher frequency of heavy vehicle interaction, occupational driving patterns, longer daily mileage, and elevated risk of material damage and liability claims from commercial fleet usage and logistics traffic.

Industrial-suburban municipality type (IS) represents transitional zones between industrial and residential areas, with lower exposure volume suggesting smaller, less dense settlements. Risk profile is attenuated relative to core industrial zones due to lower traffic intensity, but retains some occupational exposure characteristics distinguishing it from purely commercial areas.

Rural circulation areas typically exhibit lower traffic density, reduced frequency of minor collisions, lower theft exposure, and less congestion-related incidents. Vehicles in rural areas tend to travel longer distances at higher speeds on open roads, which may increase severity per claim but overall claim frequency is lower due to fewer interactions with other vehicles and pedestrians.

Urban circulation areas are associated with higher traffic density, increased frequency of low-speed collisions, higher theft and vandalism rates, greater pedestrian and cyclist interaction, and more complex road environments. These factors drive higher claim frequency, making urban zones a distinct and elevated risk segment in Non-Life motor pricing models.
